words: MARTHA LOWERY LONG: How do you want this started? Do you want it to be just
what I remember or family- my sister had a lot of family anecdotes
in there that I just know real well, but I just know them from lis
tening to the family tell them.

speaker: SS
words: Well, those that seem interesting I think, but really your own ex

speaker: SS
words: perience most of all. What you remember it being like there when

speaker: SS
words: you were a kid. You say it was rough.

speaker: MLL
words: There was no money in those early days, pioneer. They came out

speaker: MLL
words: there and settled and a dollar was just a fortune. There was no actual

speaker: MLL
words: cash. They raised what food they could and they had to buy a

speaker: MLL
words: few things. And they brought what they had with them when they came

speaker: MLL
words: to settle those homesteads. The fact that it was the Quincy Valley,

speaker: MLL
words: which was a sagebrush country, you see then, a flat, sagebrush co

speaker: MLL
words: vered desert country, and later became this lushirrigated country

speaker: MLL
words: that you drove through. That's why Quincy is interesting to a lot

speaker: MLL
words: of people. They don't know what it was like in that early day when

speaker: MLL
words: my folks- it was opened up for homestead- that's how they happened

speaker: MLL
words: to go there.

speaker: SS
words: How was it that your folks actually happened to go there?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, they had lived in North Dakota and Dad was a carpenter, I guess,

speaker: MLL
words: And North Dakota was a meadow and prairie country, level prairie coun

speaker: MLL
words: try. And my mother believed that a family was better off g H„,.w up

speaker: MLL
words: away from relatives rather than too close, although she was very de

speaker: MLL
words: voted to her relatives. And so, this Quincy Valley in the Big Bend

speaker: MLL
words: Country in Central Washington was opened up for homesteading. And

speaker: MLL
words: you could go out and select a quarter section of land and if you made

speaker: MLL
words: Certain improvements and lived on it a certain length of time, I

speaker: MLL
words: don't know the exact length of time, then you owned it. And that's

speaker: MLL
words: what they did. Dad went out first and chose this- it was a mile and

speaker: MLL
words: a half directly north of Quincy, the town of Quincy, there wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: much town yet, but there was a town, small town with a store there

speaker: MLL
words: then. And he chose the southwest quarter section. It was all square,

speaker: MLL
words: it wasn't like this country where sections are irregular; they were

speaker: MLL
words: all laid out square because it was level country. And he started to

speaker: MLL
words: build a little house. It only had one room at first, one large room

speaker: MLL
words: and it had board and batten, that's boards that go up and down and

speaker: MLL
words: ;the cracks are covered with another board, and a tar paper roof and a

speaker: MLL
words: stovepipe. It had no chinmney; there was no bricks. And when Mother

speaker: MLL
words: first came in- he was there a year building this little house; getting

speaker: MLL
words: started, and then Mother and the four older children came and these

speaker: MLL
words: cracks were still open- my sister told that in that other story- how

speaker: MLL
words: I'd been

speaker: MLL
words: scared she was that somebody'd peek in with those cracks. scared

speaker: MLL
words: too. Anyway- I was the first child born on the homestead. And I was

speaker: MLL
words: the biggest and healthiest and I still have the best health, and the

speaker: MLL
words: others are all gone except two sisters- and I've always laid it to that

speaker: MLL
words: virgin soil. They broke up this- and from my study of nutrition, I

speaker: MLL
words: think this might have had something to do with it- my health, don't

speaker: MLL
words: you?

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN LONG: They found her one day out there-

speaker: MLL
words: Now Marvin! Well, let me tell it.

speaker: SS
words: What is the story?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, we had a doctor- my sister got this part wrong- his name was

speaker: MLL
words: Dr. Widby, and he was an eye specialist in Wenatchee, later when I

speaker: MLL
words: lived in Wenatchee I knew him. But at the time he was a young country doctor. And

speaker: MLL
words: so, when the baby was- there were no hospitals or

speaker: MLL
words: anything, and when it was time for the baby to arrive why Dad got on

speaker: MLL
words: one of the plow horses, we didn't have a riding horse, you know, just

speaker: MLL
words: work horses; got on one of the horses and rode to town to get the doc

speaker: MLL
words: tor. And the doctor borrowed a saddle horse from Jack Gaither, it was

speaker: MLL
words: a bit lively, and they started out to our farm, and the horse stepped

speaker: MLL
words: in a badger hole and threw the doctor, and the horse wasn't hurt but

speaker: MLL
words: he ran back to town and the doctor's bag settled there, and the doc

speaker: MLL
words: tor picked up his bag and had to walk the rest of the way. So they

speaker: MLL
words: always said the doctor got me out of the badger hole! And on the way

speaker: MLL
words: to school later when I was school age and I walked to school through

speaker: MLL
words: the sagebrush here was this big badger hole right by the trail, and I'd

speaker: MLL
words: go around- I can still remember looking down it every time I went past

speaker: MLL
words: thinking there must be another baby in that hole! I sure that was

speaker: MLL
words: the one. I never found any babies in that badger hole.

speaker: SS
words: Did he get there in time?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, he got there in time and I was a ten pound baby. I had a good

speaker: MLL
words: start, and I kept it up all my life. And I was healthy and I think

speaker: MLL
words: it was because they were on virgin soil and that soil was producing

speaker: MLL
words: good vegetables. I don't the badger hole had anything to do with my

speaker: MLL
words: You

speaker: MLL
words: health, but the virgin soil might have. know very well it could have

speaker: MLL
words: although my mother's healthy condition, of course, did, too contributed.

speaker: MLL
words: Anyway, years later, I was working in Wenatchee- this is going ahead in

speaker: MLL
words: the story- but Dr. Widby went to Vienna and became an eye specialist.

speaker: MLL
words: And Dr. Widby fitted my first glasses.

speaker: SS
words: In Wenatchee?

speaker: MLL
words: In Wenatchee. And one time- I was in extension work at that time, and

speaker: MLL
words: the county agent belonged to Rotary Club along with this doctor, and

speaker: MLL
words: I told him- my father came to visit and he told him this story

speaker: MLL
words: about Dr. Widby delivering my mother when I was born, and oh, he

speaker: MLL
words: couldn't wait. So one time I was at Rotary Club as a guest with 4H

speaker: MLL
words: Clubs, you know or on the program. And I knew something was coming

speaker: MLL
words: but I didn't guess what it was. And he said, "I have an important

speaker: MLL
words: guest to introduce." And he asked me to stand and then he said"l'd

speaker: MLL
words: like for Dr. Widby to stand." And he told that whole Rotary Club at

speaker: MLL
words: Wenatchee that- And Dr. Widby was a small man and very much liked in

speaker: MLL
words: Wenatchee- that Dr. Widby delivered me when he was a young prac

speaker: MLL
words: titioner at Quincy. I liked 'em both so I didn't care, but it was

speaker: MLL
words: real funny.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you know, in just glancing at your sister's story, I have the

speaker: SS
words: idea that from ' that she gives of your mother, that it was

speaker: SS
words: somewhat rough on her. The living in that time.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, yes, it was- of course, all pioneer women- most pioneer women,

speaker: MLL
words: had it- but they were happy doing it. I mean, my mother knew how tofor

speaker: MLL
words: us to have great fun. Oh, my childhood was just happy and we had

speaker: MLL
words: great fun as a family. There were finally seven children; there were

speaker: MLL
words: two more after me. Two more born there.

speaker: ML
words: I was born up here in Kendrick- Avery- and when I was born there was

speaker: ML
words: women come in and took care of me.

speaker: MLL
words: But there wasn't any money, and they broke up this sagebrush and expec

speaker: MLL
words: ted to raise crops like they had in North Dakots where there was more

speaker: MLL
words: rainfall. Well, the first few years there was enough rainfall so that

speaker: MLL
words: times were not quite so hard, and they got crops and all- wheat crops.

speaker: MLL
words: And then the climate changed in that country and it became drought and

speaker: MLL
words: they weren't able to even raise a crop. And so then, my father had to

speaker: MLL
words: work out at whatever he could get. In the hills north of Quincy there

speaker: MLL
words: were wheat ranches that did produce and my Uncle Jim lived there and he

speaker: MLL
words: used to go over and work in harvest and earn money that way. Then there

speaker: MLL
words: was another way that they earned money that was sort of interesting.

speaker: MLL
words: They had- what do they call it? A road tax. They taxed everybody to

speaker: MLL
words: build the roads in those days, and if you couldn't pay your tax- if

speaker: MLL
words: you didn't want to pay your tax, you could hire somebody else to do

speaker: MLL
words: the work for you. And he did that kind of work. And he'd be gone all

speaker: MLL
words: week. He used to roll up a bed that was real heavy quilts On Sunday

speaker: MLL
words: nights we'd help him roll up bed and then heM load the wagon and the I'd

speaker: MLL
words: go out. Sometimes the boys had these jobs, too, and they'd go off and

speaker: MLL
words: work on the road all week and then come back. They'd do their own coo

speaker: MLL
words: king and sleep under the wagon if it rained a little; didn't rain much

speaker: MLL
words: in that country, but if it did. And that's the way they made a living.

speaker: ML
words: That's the way they paid taxes, too.

speaker: MLL
words: The only people that survived the dpughts- we moved away- and the farm

speaker: MLL
words: went for taxes finally because of this long drought period. And it was

speaker: MLL
words: these German people that had moved in there that were thrifty, that knew

speaker: MLL
words: how to survive. They would take- the Russian Thistles had come in as

speaker: MLL
words: one of the pests, the rail brought it, and they're the things you

speaker: MLL
words: see on Western pictures, the big tumbleweeds that go down in the ghost

speaker: MLL
words: towns, you know, a lot of those were Russian Thistles, I think. Ours

speaker: MLL
words: were big like that. And they used to cut these in the green stage and

speaker: MLL
words: stack them and feed them to their cattle. These German people knew how

speaker: MLL
words: to do that and they could survive, but we didn't; we moved. My mother

speaker: MLL
words: was determined we would have an education so we moved to Pullman.

speaker: MLL
words: But I was going to tell you about some of the fun we used to have.

speaker: MLL
words: We used to play games, you know, family. And we played lots of

speaker: MLL
words: games sitting around the big dining room table. It always had white

speaker: MLL
words: oilcloth on it. We had a big table, that would seat all nine of us,

speaker: MLL
words: of course the youngest children didn't play much. We played games like

speaker: MLL
words: not cards! My mother was against cards; against liquor and cards, base

speaker: MLL
words: ball on Sunday, and she had very strict standards about those things.

speaker: MLL
words: A lot of people did in those days. You were either one way or you were

speaker: MLL
words: the other. So we didn't know anything about baseball because it was al

speaker: MLL
words: ways played on Sunday and you couldn't play games on Sunday, like that.

speaker: MLL
words: We couldn't play with a ball at home on Sunday, even. We couldn't cut

speaker: MLL
words: paper dolls on Sunday. I've never understood that, but there was a hook

speaker: MLL
words: on the wall where we kept the scissors- and the paper dolls were a big

speaker: MLL
words: thing in our life, but Sunday you couldn't take down the scissors and

speaker: MLL
words: cut paper dolls. Sunday had to be special. And that impressed us.

speaker: MLL
words: We went to church. We got ready on Saturday. And no work was ever

speaker: MLL
words: done on the farm on Sunday. My people were Presbyterians.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN: We never had that kind of thing

speaker: MLL
words: Well, that's the way it was in those early days. And there wasn't any

speaker: MLL
words: work done on Sunday. The horses were allowed to take yo u to church,

speaker: MLL
words: but the horses weren't worked, they rested, too. And then the preacheroh,

speaker: MLL
words: we had a big dinner. We got up and had to kill a chicken- there

speaker: MLL
words: wasn't any refrigeration, so you had to get up and kill the chickens

speaker: MLL
words: Sunday morning and get it started cooking. And then bring the preacher

speaker: MLL
words: home for dinner. And my mother worked hard, but then that was all in—

speaker: MLL
words: for the good of the Lord, because it was the preacher feeding,

speaker: MLL
words: you know.

speaker: SS
words: Did he come to dinner often?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, he came to dinner often. The preacher was just like a member of

speaker: MLL
words: our family. He was always one of my mother's best friends. And the

speaker: MLL
words: first preacher I remember was a- they called him- he was quite well

speaker: MLL
words: known in that country. They called him- what did they call him? He

speaker: MLL
words: was like an itinerate preacher- they called him the jack rabbit preacher

speaker: MLL
words: because jack rabbits were prevalent in the country and he came from

speaker: MLL
words: one town to the other.

speaker: SS
words: Circuit rider?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. On that order. And he used to ride a bicycle. The Great Nor

speaker: MLL
words: thern Railroad went through Quincy, and he would ride that bicycle

speaker: MLL
words: down that railroad, just imagine, bumpity bump, from the little towns-

speaker: MLL
words: Not Soap Lake- but the next little town, Adrian, I guess it was up the

speaker: MLL
words: road. He'd ride to Quincy to get there in time for the service on

speaker: MLL
words: this bicycle! And then Mother would take him home to dinner. He wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: married; he had been married and he would have had a daughter my age,

speaker: MLL
words: so he was very fond of me, and I just adored him. As a child, I still

speaker: MLL
words: remember him. His name was Herbert M. Course. And he was a big, lanky

speaker: MLL
words: man. But everybody just ltWed him. And she'd bring him home to dinner.

speaker: MLL
words: I mean, we would. And then she'd cook dinner; cook chicken dinner for

speaker: MLL
words: him.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what happened to his family?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, his wife died at childbirth. And so, he married later and we

speaker: MLL
words: kept in touch with him and I knew his second family years later. When

speaker: MLL
words: he left Quincy, I was just a child, just quite small,I don't know how

speaker: MLL
words: old I was, nine- no, six or seven years old, I suppose. And so, he

speaker: MLL
words: said, because of my two older sisters, he said, "When you get ready to

speaker: MLL
words: get married I'll come back and marry you." Oh, me, it was me he was

speaker: MLL
words: talking too, because I was his little favorite, so I always talked about

speaker: MLL
words: when I was ten, Mr. Course was going to come back and marry me. Of

speaker: MLL
words: course by the time I was that old, I realized what he meant. He didn't

speaker: MLL
words: mean he was going to marry me, he was going to perform the ceremony

speaker: MLL
words: But he neyer got to do that,

speaker: MLL
words: for all of us. ^But he was quite a talented and interesting man.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that religion was particularly important to your mother?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, it was their life. It was why they could do these things. Why

speaker: MLL
words: they could take up homesteads. They had to have it;.depended on it.

speaker: MLL
words: They had faith, you see. They knew that it would come out alright,

speaker: MLL
words: and it did. Everything came out alright. Four of her children gradua

speaker: MLL
words: ted from WSC up here. That was her aim in life, to have her children

speaker: MLL
words: educated. The boys didn't graduate, but the girls all did.

speaker: SS
words: Were the women- this I am a little curious about- were the women more

speaker: SS
words: involved in the church than the men?

speaker: MLL
words: Not in our family. My father was- he was very musical - he was an Irishhis

speaker: MLL
words: parents had come from Ireland and he had an Irish tenor voice, and

speaker: MLL
words: he always led the singing in the Sunday School and church and sang in

speaker: MLL
words: the choir, and was a superintendent of the Sunday School. And my mo

speaker: MLL
words: ther was so busy having children that he did more work, I think, that

speaker: MLL
words: way than she did, but she was back of him in all of that,

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: Our family— my father, of course, there was a big family of us and

speaker: MLL
words: Mother had to-

speaker: MLL
words: They had to do the work at home.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: had to do the work at home.

speaker: MLL
words: But she saw to it that we were always clean and dressed

speaker: MLL
words: MAEVIN L: But we always had to go to church.

speaker: MLL
words: Made over handed down clothes. You know, everything was recycled

speaker: MLL
words: in those days, but we didn't call it that. Nothing was ever wasted.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: Everybody went to church.

speaker: MLL
words: Yeah. We all went to church. And we never missed. I've got a little

speaker: MLL
words: gold pin that I got for perfect attendance at Sunday School. I've still

speaker: MLL
words: got my little gold pin that I earned.

speaker: SS
words: Weren't you able to play any games on Sunday?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes, we could play with our dolls, but we couldn't— arid we often

speaker: MLL
words: had company and we could play- go out and play. But it was limited

speaker: MLL
words: somewhat. We had to know it was Sunday. And then sitting around in

speaker: MLL
words: your Sunday clothes, that alane was a bit of a hardship, you know,

speaker: MLL
words: when you wanted to have on your play clothes. And you kept them on

speaker: MLL
words: if you had company, and so that made Sunday kind of a stuffnecked

speaker: MLL
words: affair, too, I think.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember what those early sermons were like?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, I don't suppose I ever heard the sermons.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, the kids didn't?

speaker: MLL
words: I'll tell you- well, children, I don't think even yet listen very much

speaker: MLL
words: to sermons- do they? They learn in the Sunday School classes- I can

speaker: MLL
words: remember things we learned in Sunday School classes. I had a lovely

speaker: MLL
words: Sunday School teacher, and I can remember the stories- Bible stories

speaker: MLL
words: if that we learned there. But I don't remember the sermons.

speaker: MLL
words: there was the singing- I know the songs that we sang. And I can re

speaker: MLL
words: member mostly what the beautiful hats and the clothes that the women

speaker: MLL
words: sitting in front of us wore! You know, and people around- the young

speaker: MLL
words: couple that held hands under the songbook, and that sort of thing.

speaker: MLL
words: We were more interested in the children- more interested in the people

speaker: MLL
words: in the church or the colored window. There was a colored memorial

speaker: MLL
words: window in this church. This preacher I was speaking of had it put in

speaker: MLL
words: in memory of his wife that died. And that church is still there in

speaker: MLL
words: Quincy. The Presbyterians built a big new church, but that little

speaker: MLL
words: church is still there.

speaker: SS
words: The Presbyterian church there- was it a community church?

speaker: MLL
words: No, it was a Presbyterian Church, but it seems to me that sometimes

speaker: MLL
words: the Methodists also- that they kind of alternated. It seems to me

speaker: MLL
words: there were years when they alternated, but it was built as a

speaker: MLL
words: Presbyterian Church. And when they first went there there was no

speaker: MLL
words: church. And they held services in a hall, they had a hall called the

speaker: MLL
words: Dickerson Hall. And it was used for all sorts of community affairs.

speaker: MLL
words: I think my sister in that story told about the big sagebrush that was

speaker: MLL
words: used as the first community Christmas tree because they didn't have

speaker: MLL
words: any evergreen trees. And later they shipped them in, but at that time

speaker: MLL
words: they used this great big sagebrush, and it stood up in the corner of

speaker: MLL
words: the hall and they decorated it just like a Christmas tree. At home

speaker: MLL
words: we had to burn sagebrush, but I don't remember that we ever took a

speaker: MLL
words: sagebrush in for— it probably was too dangerous, because they burned

speaker: MLL
words: easily. But we did burn sagebrush. And sometimes up in the drawsdo

speaker: MLL
words: you know what a draw is? We called it a little ravine- was called

speaker: MLL
words: a draw- spring runoff would wash the little-^ they called them draws in

speaker: MLL
words: that country. And the sagebrush that grew in these draws, up in the

speaker: MLL
words: lower part of the hills grew very large. Sometimes as large as six or

speaker: MLL
words: eight inches across the trunk. But around where we lived were

speaker: MLL
words: smaller, but those big ones made good fuel and my dad would take the

speaker: MLL
words: wagon and go up there in the fall and get a load of that for fuel. In

speaker: MLL
words: later years they shipped in slabwood and we burned that, but the early

speaker: MLL
words: days they burned sagebrush, so you see there was no expense, it was

speaker: MLL
words: just labor.

speaker: SS
words: It would take a lot more work when they could cut up sagebrush to

speaker: SS
words: burn-

speaker: MLL
words: Well, you know it's soft, so it isn't hard to cut and they just cut it

speaker: MLL
words: with an axe. This big stuff they probably had to saw, when they found

speaker: MLL
words: those big ones. I don't know, I don't remember that. One time Dad and

speaker: MLL
words: the boys, the two older brothers went with him, and+heytook me, I was

speaker: MLL
words: sort of the tomboy and I liked to go more and there was two girls at

speaker: MLL
words: home to help with the dishes and things, so I never relly did much

speaker: MLL
words: housework then, and I still don't. (Chuckles) But they took me with

speaker: MLL
words: them and it was an allday trip with the wagon up into the hills to get

speaker: MLL
words: it and we took our lunch. And I remember we cooked out. That was my

speaker: MLL
words: first cookout. And Dad took potatoes and onions and built a fire and

speaker: MLL
words: we roasted those. Oh, I can still taste them and were they ever good!

speaker: MLL
words: And I was just a little girl, you know* and I played. And there was

speaker: MLL
words: big grass, Dad called it rye grass that grew around where they were

speaker: MLL
words: cutting the sagebrush, and after the manner of children I built me a

speaker: MLL
words: hut, you know children go through that stage of building all kinds of

speaker: MLL
words: houses at different ages, you know. And I built a hut out of this rye

speaker: MLL
words: grass. And of course, it probably was nothing, but to me it was quite

speaker: MLL
words: elegant- Then they would pile this sagebrush up in the yard by the

speaker: MLL
words: cellar, a safe distance from the house. One time it caught on fire

speaker: MLL
words: from a spark and we lost it and he had to go back and get a second sup

speaker: MLL
words: ply. That was tragic, of course, all that extra work. But you know

speaker: MLL
words: it burns real quick and hot.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: My grandparents, uncles and aunts was pioneers in the Yakima Valley

speaker: MLL
words: sagebrush. Cleared the land they had.

speaker: MLL
words: The land at Quincy was real sandy and light, and all it needed was

speaker: MLL
words: water.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: — sandy and every house there had to be well plastered.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, our house wasn't plastered, and when there was a sandstorm co

speaker: MLL
words: ming, Mother would say, "Children, run and close the doors." But

speaker: MLL
words: it was no use the sand would come right in anyway, you couldn't keep

speaker: MLL
words: it out. Those awful dust storms^they had in that country, they still

speaker: MLL
words: have them. Now of course with irrigation it isn't so bad, but south

speaker: MLL
words: of Quincy they still have 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Well how were the slats tightened up.

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, well, board and batten- that means boards that are put together

speaker: MLL
words: as close as possible, but there is still a crack and then a board is

speaker: MLL
words: put on the outside over that crack. And that's the kind of construe

speaker: MLL
words: tion it was. That was homestead houses- were built that way. And^some

speaker: MLL
words: times they had- I don't think our house ever had anything but a tar pa

speaker: MLL
words: per roof. It had to be replaced often, of course. I think it always

speaker: MLL
words: ways castor oil. And you didn't try to show you had a little stomach

speaker: MLL
words: ache, because if you got it, you got a dose of castor oil! And it was

words: drastic! And that was one of the common ones and everybody used it.

speaker: MLL
words: And then for cuts and small inuries of that kind, there was turpentine,

words: which was used in our family a great deal. That would heal up anything.

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: in a way. She would take the big rocking chair and put a big quilt in

speaker: MLL
words: it and then the blankets- the wash blankets- and put you in your night

speaker: MLL
words: gown in that chair and take a five gallon oil can- we always had those they

speaker: MLL
words: were salvage too, the ones that the kerosene came in. The top

speaker: MLL
words: was cut out carefully so that it made a container. And you put your

speaker: MLL
words: feet in that with hot water and mustard, and you soaked your feet in

speaker: MLL
words: that, and you sat is this chair all wrapped up in this blanket and

speaker: MLL
words: drank hot lemonades and then you would sweat. And that took everything

speaker: MLL
words: out of you that you didn't have as well as what you did have! You never

speaker: MLL
words: had a cold afterwards. And then as soon as you thought you'd been there

speaker: MLL
words: long enough, and you'd sweat enough, why she would get the washtub out

speaker: MLL
words: and have the room good and hot and give you a bath and put you in bed

speaker: MLL
words: till you cooled off, because you know it's dangerous to get chilled.

speaker: MLL
words: That was a standard remedy. And oh, a mustard plaster on your chest

speaker: MLL
words: and one on your back. And they were hot and they blistered you. And

speaker: MLL
words: they were homemade. Made out of the same mustard that you  jk

words: on your ham. And you made that by mixing it up and sometimes you put

speaker: MLL
words: a little flour in that and that reduced the heat so it wasn't quite so

speaker: MLL
words: bad. But she'd take a piece of an old sheet and stir up this mustard

speaker: MLL
words: and water and lather it out on the sheet; fold it up and put that on

speaker: MLL
words: you, put a flannel over it and that would sure cure a cold! (Chuckles)

speaker: MLL
words: again, she saiid,"you say, I'm Irish." And they'll say, "Alright, talk

words: some." And then you say, "I'm Irish, and if you don't believe it, I

words: can show it to you, I have it in me pocket." And then you pull out th is

words: more. (Laughter) So, we're proud of being Irish. My dad could sing

words: Yes, the playing cards. I'd never seen playing cards. We had Flinch

speaker: MLL
words: and we played Muggins with the flinch cards. We played card games, we

speaker: MLL
words: played old maid, and authors was great. But not playing cards, because

speaker: MLL
words: playing cards were associated with the devil in the religious training

speaker: MLL
words: in those early days. And gambling- you know, you couldn't play cards

speaker: MLL
words: and not become a gambler, that was the supposition. So I'd never seen

speaker: MLL
words: playing cards, I'd just seen these flinch cards which were- oh, they

speaker: MLL
words: weren't bright colored, they were gray-green and white or brown-white

speaker: MLL
words: or maybe sometimes of rosy and white. They were pretty designs

speaker: MLL
words: on the cards that we had. And of course, old maid cards had pictures

speaker: MLL
words: on them. Authors cards had pictures. When I was four years old my

speaker: MLL
words: mother was to go back to North Dakota to see her folks and I went with

speaker: MLL
words: her because a child that age could travel without any fare. The other

speaker: MLL
words: children were in school and I was the youngest, so I went with my mother.

speaker: MLL
words: And on the train in those days they had a table- they would bring you

speaker: MLL
words: a table that was put up between the seats. The seats would- plush

speaker: MLL
words: seats that faced each other, and there was a table between and you could

speaker: MLL
words: eat your lunch; you carried your lunch in a shoe box and you could eat

speaker: MLL
words: your lunch on that taM e or you could play games or whatever you wished.

speaker: MLL
words: And so the people in the seat behind us were playing cards, and I stood

speaker: MLL
words: up in the seat and looked over, and the whole thing was very exciting

speaker: MLL
words: to me. I don't remember much when I was four, but I remembered this be

speaker: MLL
words: cause it was all different you know, see, the train and the plush seats,

speaker: MLL
words: red plush seats, and standing up and then we were going to see Grandpa

speaker: MLL
words: and Grandma, besides, which was great excitement. And I stood up and

speaker: MLL
words: these people were playing with these beautiful cards and I was so ex

speaker: MLL
words: cited. And I said, "Mother, look, come and look." And she looked and

speaker: MLL
words: she yanked me down, and boy, "No, you mustn't ever look at those, you

speaker: MLL
words: must never play with those." She said. And I didn't know why and I

speaker: MLL
words: didn't understand why. I just knew they were beautiful and those people

speaker: MLL
words: were having fun, and it's too bad it couldn't have been explained bet

speaker: MLL
words: ter. I didn't learn to play cards til I was through college and out in

speaker: MLL
words: the world. My mother was gone by then, and she probably would have

speaker: MLL
words: changed by then too, and I became a good card player.

speaker: SS

words: Yes, I learned to dance in college. But I had to take lessons though

speaker: MLL
words: because I was I was so old by that time that everybody else knew how.

speaker: MLL
words: They learned, you know, along the way, like people are supposed to learn

speaker: MLL
words: things, but I hadn't so I had to go to Saturday afternoon,- on the cam

speaker: MLL
words: pus, they had a class because there were lots of college kids brought

speaker: MLL
words: up just like I'd been that didn't know how to dance. And we learned to

speaker: MLL
words: dance.

words: checkers and those things that you played in between tw o or three or

words: don't remember going to many of those in the town. The traveling was a

words: little too difficult, but town families get together and have em.

words: We played games like- oh, you could play games that were almost dancing

words: like Farmer-in-the-Dell, and Skip-to-my-Lou. We played those even in

words: And we played Spin-the-Platter ahd Winkum, and Post Office. My mother

speaker: SS

words: game. But Winkum was okay. She'd let us play Winkum, but not Post Of

speaker: SS
words: fice. That was off the list!(Chuckles)

speaker: MLL
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: Those party songs, were they part of the-?

words: ML1: Anything- Skip-to-my-Lou you had a partner, it was really dancing like

words: folk dancing. You had a partner, you held ha nds and you

words: lowed but not social dancing.

words: loons and the rough life that they were trying to protect us from.

words: at public dances. And so, you would be led into this temptation, you

words: see, and that's why it was prohibited, and we didn't do it.

speaker: MLL
words: Famer-in-the-Dell. That was fun, because you chose you the farmer

words: chose his wife, see. If you got to be his wife, that was a big night.

speaker: MLL
words: You know, if it was your favorite boy. Those are kid games, but adults

speaker: MLL
words: games.

words: MARVIN L: Did you ever play Skip-to-my-Lou?

words: And then Spin the Platter. You had a circle and then somebody was "It"

words: and you spun the platter, and if you could grab it before it stopped,

words: then you were "IT". Had a lot of games that were fun.

words: travel, I think. And a lot of going to Sunday dinner. They were too

words: families would get together.

words: and she didn't use it as she would have. And that was a papered house

words: and it had big pink roses on the wallpaper and oh, I thought that was

speaker: MLL
words: just the end. That beautiful pink roses patterned wallpaper! We

speaker: MLL
words: didn't have any wallpaper on our walls. I thought to have a house like

words: that they must be wealthy, just very wealthy. And she had an ostrich

words: sures. And one time for Easter, she took an ostrich- not an ostrich-

words: I'm sorry, I meant a peacock- she would give us the peacock feathers,

words: they were so beautiful. And a peacock egg is quite large- not as

speaker: MLL
words: large as an ostrich- well, she didn t have an ostrich- she had peacock .

words: And one Easter she had-boiled a peacock egg and colored it and gave it

speaker: MLL
words: to me for a present and I kept it. My mother let me keep it. I kept it

speaker: MLL
words: upstairs in the loft. Of course, I suppose it finally dried up instead

speaker: MLL
words: of- it rotted probably. It was one of my treasures I

words: is actually so much bigger than a duck egg. Is it?

words: I don't know, probably twice as big as a chicken egg.

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: Well, you get an idea how we lived anyway, haven't you?

speaker: MLL
words: you got free, you know, was made-fun. We used baking soda, lots of

speaker: MLL
words: baking soda in cooking and then in it was also a home remedy for upset

speaker: MLL
words: stomachs and bee stings and all kinds of things. It still is a good

speaker: MLL
words: remedy. But in the baking soda package there was a little card- lit

speaker: MLL
words: tle birds. And it was a little premium or a little gift with the pac

speaker: MLL
words: kage. And then we collected those. And when we bought a new package

speaker: MLL
words: of baking soda, which cost a nickel, this little card was attached to

speaker: MLL
words: it, and it was someone's turn to have it that time. You know, you

speaker: MLL
words: couldn't always get the card yourself, but you would save those and

speaker: MLL
words: then you would have a collection of beautiful pictures of birds. And

speaker: MLL
words: now, they would be an antique collection if anyone has any of those.

speaker: MLL
words: You keep your eye out, because you might find them.

speaker: MLL
words: less than a dime.

speaker: MLL
words: A dime? I thought it was a nickel. You sure? Well, you're the mer

speaker: MLL
words: chant, so you'd know. But anyway, baking soda went a long ways. We

words: biscuits. These men that went out to work on the roads did their own

words: cooking and they made soda biscuits. And they learned to make awful

words: good ones, and so when they came home then our treat was for the menmy

speaker: MLL
words: brothers and Dad to make the biscuits for supper. They were crisp,

speaker: MLL
words: had crust. Soda biscuit has a nice crust.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: We generally had them of course for breakfast. Mother, toast, pancakes-

speaker: MLL
words: But you always had biscuits.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: Always had biscuits.

speaker: MLL
words: But we didn't. My mother didn't think so much baking powder bread was

speaker: MLL
words: good for you.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: She always had biscuits. Dad had to have biscuits.

speaker: MLL
words: And see that was the difference between New England and Southern— his

speaker: MLL
words: mother was a Southern cook, and she always made biscuits. My mother

speaker: MLL
words: was New England and they were raised on yeast bread, and she didn't

speaker: MLL
words: think so much baking powder bread was good for you, so we just had it

speaker: MLL
words: sort of like emergency bread when you ran out of yeast bread, then you

speaker: SS
words: How hard do you feel that your parents had to work?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, they worked hard. They got up early and there was tock to take

speaker: MLL
words: care of, and the farming to do. Any everything had to be made. Clo

speaker: MLL
words: thes had to be made. Butchering. The butchering, you know, was hard

speaker: MLL
words: work. And everything was made and there was no refrigeration, so

speaker: MLL
words: butchering was a problem to keep the meat. In the winter it wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: any problem.

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: The children helped. Everybody worked.

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: Yeah, you milked the cow twice a day, and then you put it in pans and

speaker: MLL
words: you put it in the cellar and the cream rose and you skimmed off the

speaker: MLL
words: was made into butter. We churned the butter in a dasher churn. The

speaker: MLL
words: children had to help do that with the dasher churn. Took a long time to

speaker: MLL
words: get the butter to come. Mother made the butter,

speaker: MLL
words: Tell about the first churn- how you churned.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, to make things easier we were taught to sing. We sang a lot when

speaker: MLL
words: we did chores like that. And we sang this, "Churn, butter, churn,-"

speaker: MLL
words: I've forgotten the rest of it- "Peter's waiting at the gate, waiting

speaker: MLL
words: for a buttered cake." And then you'd sing this little verse and that

speaker: MLL
words: made the time go faster. And then we finally had the washing machineat

speaker: MLL
words: first, of course, it was just a washboard, but then we got a washing

speaker: MLL
words: machine that had a wheel that had to be turned. It didn't get the

speaker: MLL
words: clothes very clean, but the boys and I had to turn it sometimes and

speaker: MLL
words: we would sing or we would read stories while we turned this and of

speaker: MLL
words: course if the story got too exciting you'd forget to turn. It wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: a very good system, but the children had to help with all these chores.

speaker: MLL
words: We gathered the eggs.

speaker: MLL

words: My mother did the reading but we all sat around at all ages and listened.

words: ly wait for the next chapter to see what happened to the Swiss Family

speaker: MLL
words: Robinson. Robinson Crusoe.

words: My goodness, and then we'd go out in the sagebrush and play act these

words: things, you know. And one time somebody sent us a box of books; I

words: kettle acooking the missionaries over the fire, in this book. And so

words: nibals, and we acted, you know, like kids do, we acted this all out!

speaker: MLL
words: fixed sort of a tent. They had a big canvas they fixed kind of

speaker: MLL

words: member the old school benches that you went up and sat on when you

speaker: MLL
words: recited- they sort of tipped a little, and of course in the night we

speaker: MLL
words: children all rolled off, She put a rope around, that was sup

speaker: MLL
words: tent, that was a sure cure. Of course later that's been proven that

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: lot of prunes and then she'd can 'em in two-quart jars; the old style

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: vegetables andt fruit from freezing. Most winters it wouldn't freeze.

speaker: MLL

words: All four of us were home economics teachers.

words: girls could do in those days, you know. You could be a nurse, you

words: could take nurse's training, which was real hard or you could teach or

speaker: MLL
words: you could get jobs in stores; derk.^ in stores, which didn't pay very

speaker: MLL
words: well, or you could be a telephone operator, but you know, there wasn'tor

speaker: MLL
words: you could do housework. But there weren't too many kinds of work

speaker: MLL
words: that women did, you know. But teachers were considered- that was con

speaker: MLL
words: sidered a good job for a girl if she could get the education, and there

speaker: MLL
words: was always a job. And then being a college graduate I could do high

speaker: MLL
words: school teaching and I taught in high school first.

speaker: SS
words: You said that you couldn't teach if you were married.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, they didn't hire very many married teachers. There were some in

speaker: MLL
words: those early schools that I taught in. There would have to be reasons

speaker: MLL
words: why. There-would have to be a great need. If you got married, you

speaker: MLL
words: just resigned right then during the school year. They didn't permit

speaker: MLL
words: that. They told teachers what to do in those days. When I took my

speaker: MLL
words: first teaching job at Culdesac down here in Idaho, it was understood

speaker: MLL
words: that I would stay there weekends, that I would work in the community.

speaker: MLL
words: That was part of your contract. I was used to that, I'd always worked

speaker: MLL
words: in the church, and I worked in the church at Culdesac, I liked it.

speaker: MLL
words: I was used to doing it; I wanted to do it. But a lot of teachers didn't

speaker: MLL
words: they lived in Lewiston and they wanted to go home, don't you see?

speaker: MLL
words: Made a problem. And they did go home and the school board didn't like

speaker: MLL
words: it. They wanted them to stay there. You weren't hired just to teach

speaker: MLL
words: you were hired to be a part of the community. And that's good; in a

speaker: MLL
words: way it's good, because a teacher's influence is greater on the kids

speaker: MLL
words: out of school than in, really.

speaker: SS
words: In the church and other activities?

speaker: MLL
words: Teachers were important people in those little towns. In little towns

speaker: MLL
words: especially. Now I don't know that that was true- in my day, a teacher

speaker: MLL
words: couldn't teach in those little towns if she smoked- if they knew she

speaker: MLL
words: smoked- or if she- I don't know, what else? She couldn't get married.

speaker: SS
words: have to do with it, one way or the other.

words: It wouldn't nowdays. Nowdays- they didn't get married til they got

speaker: MLL
words: through college- it was partly the economics, they couldn't afford to.

speaker: MLL
words: There was no way that a couple could get married and both go to college.

speaker: MLL
words: It just couldn't be done. And the girls weren't trained so they

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: college like they do now. And they didn't get married as teachers.

speaker: MLL

speaker: MLL
words: scarce and hard to get, probably. I don't know. But there had to be

speaker: MLL
words: exceptions. There were plenty of unmarried teachers that needed jobs,

speaker: MLL
words: was the reason, I think. They needed the jobs, and they just wouldn't

speaker: MLL
words: hire married women.

speaker: SS
words: When you started going to WSC, what was it like? What was the program

speaker: SS
words: that they had for you?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, it was called home economics. Earlier it had been called domes

speaker: MLL
words: tic science; not in my day. It had come a long ways by then. Dean

speaker: MLL
words: Harrison was a wonderful woman and it was a small school; Pullman was

speaker: MLL
words: a small town then. And the college was small compared to what it is

speaker: MLL
words: now; that was in 1920, I graduated from high school in 1920 and I star

speaker: MLL
words: ted college that fall. And there was nutrition, and there was cooking,

speaker: MLL
words: still called cooking, and sewing. A lot of clothing work and some of

speaker: MLL
words: it was actual sewing. You had to learn all the fundamentals of sewing

speaker: MLL
words: and patterns and fitting and making clothes, because you still made

speaker: MLL
words: clothes. We learned to make hats; still made hats. And there was

speaker: MLL
words: tailoring; more advanced work. Then you had to take chemistry. There

speaker: MLL
words: was a lot of science. It was considered one of the hardest courses

speaker: MLL
words: that women took. Pharmacy was the hardest, I guess, that girls took.

speaker: MLL
words: And home economics was one of the next because of the science. There

speaker: MLL
words: was two years of chemistry, including organic and bacteriology and

speaker: MLL
words: zoology. Those were all laboratory. There was lots of laboratory work

speaker: MLL
words: to it, you see, as well as all your home economics classes had laborator. So it was

speaker: MLL
words: hard in that respect. And there were very few electives.

speaker: MLL
words: And the few electives that were- you had to take general courses

speaker: MLL
words: of course, like English and, let's see- no mathematics. Didn't take

speaker: MLL
words: any mathematics or history. Didn't have to have any of that, but you

speaker: MLL
words: had to take English. And physical education was a requirement for

speaker: MLL
words: everybody. There was no intramurals, it was just between classes; the

speaker: MLL
words: games. But you played the games, basketball and hockey- girls' hockey.

words: cian and work in hospital. You could specialize in that. Or you

speaker: MML

speaker: MML
words: see, what else? In those days, those were two fields. There weren't

speaker: MML
words: as many as there are now.

words: Oh, yes, yes, lots of them^get married before they finished. That was

speaker: MLL
words: beneficial, of course. But a lot of them became teachers and then this

speaker: MLL
words: They usually went into that from teaching, though. They usually taught

speaker: MLL
words: first to get experience. I don't know very many that went directly in

words: to extension. That was teaching, of course, in a way. And then there

speaker: MLL
words: buyers

words: was some commercial fields. There was some in textiles. Textile

words: and textile manufacturers hired home economic trained people. So they

words: specialized in textile work if they were interested in that. There

speaker: MLL

words: textiles. That was laboratory work in our course, too. It was a good

speaker: MLL
words: could be Canned in a pint jar.

speaker: MLL
words: I was supposed to teach these girls to

speaker: MLL
words: fit a chicken into a- I knew how to do it- I knew how to do that, but

speaker: MLL
words: a big hen couldn't be fitted into- there was no way a big hen could be

speaker: MLL
words: fitted into a pint jar. But we only had a certain amount of time for

speaker: MLL
words: the class and it was held clear across town in the Pullman High School

speaker: MLL
words: so the kids had to come from the campus, clear across town to Pullman

speaker: MLL
words: High School laboratory where this- where I was teaching this class,

speaker: MLL
words: and these chickens were supposed to be all cut up ready for us to can.

speaker: MLL
words: The cutting was not supposed to be part of the lesson. And so that

speaker: MLL
words: morning when I arrived and the chickens arrived from the poultry depar

speaker: MLL
words: tment they were dressed but they weren't cut up and they were very large,

speaker: MLL
words: And I only had a certain amount of time for this class; they had to

speaker: MLL
words: be cut up; packed in the jars and processed and let those kids get back

speaker: MLL
words: to the campus: it was an impossibility. So, we started in

words: these big chickens; they couldn't get a whole chicken in a jar, which

speaker: MLL
words: was what I had hoped to teach them, but we did the best we could, and

speaker: MLL
words: we canned the chickens, and I spent the rest of the day finishing up

words: that chicken. I had to stay in that laboratory all afternoon pressure

speaker: MLL
words: cooking the rest of these chickens, because the girls didn't have time

speaker: MLL
words: to finish. They each did a jar. We got through that. But all these

words: and that chicken is all canned and its here and I hope it will keep."

words: "But," I said, "I wouldn't guarantee that it'll keep." And she laughed.

words: Is there anything from the early days that you can think of that we

speaker: MLL
words: Most of what I've told him. Lee and I have always sat and visited and

words: I never thought about putting it down. But it's fun to remember. We

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L: husband. (Chuckles)

speaker: MLL
words: Because of what I was when I met you. I was already made then because

speaker: MLL
words: a child has to be formed. Strictness doesn't hurt a child. But fun,

speaker: MLL
words: they have to have fun; my mother believed in fun. And beauty; lots of

speaker: MLL
words: beauty. Everything had to be beautiful. It was hard in those days.

speaker: MLL
words: We planted- one time we got some tulips. Some way, I don't know how

speaker: MLL
words: we got these tulips, we planted a bed of tulips. And everybody had one

speaker: MLL
words: tulip. Mine was a pink striped one. That was the way she made things
